items.explanationShort:
librairie (FR) = a bookstore, a shop that sells books.
library (EN) = a place where you borrow books.
Both trace back to Latin 'liber' (book), but French kept the selling side.

actuellement (FR) = currently, right now.
actually (EN) = in truth, in fact, for emphasis or correction.

éventuellement (FR) = possibly, perhaps.
eventually (EN) = in the end, after some time.
French expresses uncertainty; English points to a final outcome.

collège (FR) = middle school in France, for ages 11-15.
college (EN) = higher education after high school.
The Napoleonic education system fixed it as the level between primary school and high school.

location (FR) = a rental, the act of renting a flat, car, or equipment.
location (EN) = a geographical place or position.
Both from Latin 'locare' (to place), but French took the leasing action while English took the place itself.

stage (FR) = an internship or training course.
stage (EN) = a platform for performances, or a phase in a process.
From Old French 'estage' (a stay); the internship sense grew from the idea of a temporary stay for learning.

chair (FR) = flesh, the soft tissue of the body.
chair (EN) = a piece of furniture you sit on.
Completely different Latin roots: French from 'caro' (flesh), English from 'cathedra' (seat).

caméra (FR) = primarily a video camera, but also used for surveillance cameras.
camera (EN) = any device that takes photos or records video.

préservatif (FR) = a condom.
preservative (EN) = a chemical added to food to keep it fresh.
A notorious source of confusion for travellers ordering food in France.

raisin (FR) = a grape, the fresh fruit on the vine.
raisin (EN) = a dried grape.
English borrowed the word from Old French when it still meant fresh grape, then applied it only to the dried version.

tissu (FR) = fabric or cloth.
tissue (EN) = thin disposable paper, or biological body tissue in science.
Both from Old French "tissu" (woven); the meanings split between textile and paper.

monnaie (FR) = loose change (coins) or a country's currency.
money (EN) = wealth in general, cash of any kind.
Both from Latin 'Moneta,' a title of goddess Juno whose temple housed Rome's coin mint.

pétrole (FR) = crude oil, the raw resource from the ground.
petrol (EN) = refined fuel for cars (gasoline in American English).

grappe (FR) = a cluster or bunch, especially of fruit on a stem.
grape (EN) = a single small fruit used for eating or making wine.
French names the whole bunch hanging together; English names the individual berry.

définitivement (FR) = permanently, for good.
definitely (EN) = certainly, without a doubt.

assister (FR) = to attend or be present at an event.
assist (EN) = to help someone.
From Latin 'assistere' (to stand by); French kept the passive witness sense, English took the active help sense.

attendre (FR) = to wait.
attend (EN) = to be present at an event.
From Latin 'attendere' (to stretch toward); French kept the waiting, English kept the showing up.

demander (FR) = to ask or request, with no aggressive tone.
demand (EN) = to insist forcefully, with authority.

quitter (FR) = to leave a place or a person.
quit (EN) = to stop doing something, especially a job or a habit.

avertir (FR) = to warn or notify someone in advance.
avert (EN) = to prevent or turn away from danger.

blesser (FR) = to injure or wound someone.
bless (EN) = to give a holy or divine favour.
English 'bless' comes from Old English 'blēdsian' (to consecrate with blood) — no relation to the French word.

charger (FR) = to load or fill — a truck, a weapon, a file.
charge (EN) = to bill for payment, accuse of a crime, or rush forward.
From Late Latin 'carricare' (to load a cart); the meanings forked as commerce and law created new needs.

exiger (FR) = a common verb meaning to require or demand.
exigent (EN) = a rare adjective meaning urgent or pressing.

médecin (FR) = a doctor, the person who treats patients.
medicine (EN) = a drug or substance used to treat illness.
Same Latin root 'medicus,' but French named the healer while English named the remedy.

lecture (FR) = reading, the act of reading a book or text.
lecture (EN) = a spoken presentation to an audience.
From Latin 'lectura' (reading); English shifted it from reading aloud to formal speaking.

journal (FR) = a newspaper, or the TV news broadcast.
journal (EN) = a personal diary, or an academic publication.
From 'jour' (day) — originally a daily record; French defaulted to the daily paper, English to the private diary.

figure = a shape, diagram, or numerical symbol.
In French it also means face. English does not share this meaning.

habit (FR) = a piece of clothing, or clothes in general.
habit (EN) = a repeated behaviour or routine.
From Latin 'habitus' which covered both dress and condition; each language picked one meaning.

travail (FR) = work or labour.
travel (EN) = the act of going on a journey.
From Latin 'tripalium' (a torture device); French kept the suffering-turned-work meaning, English shifted to journeys.

coin (FR) = a corner or a spot.
coin (EN) = a small metal disc used as money.
French 'coin' comes from Latin 'cuneus' (wedge), describing the angular shape of a corner.

phrase (FR) = a complete sentence in French.
phrase (EN) = a group of words or expression, usually shorter than a full sentence.

entrée (FR) = the starter or first course of a meal.
entrée (EN) = the main dish in American English.
Same word, opposite position on the menu.

supporter (FR) = to tolerate or endure something unpleasant.
support (EN) = to help, encourage, or hold something up.

résumer (FR) = to summarize or condense.
resume (EN) = to continue after a pause.

actuel (FR) = current, present-day.
actual (EN) = real, true, not imagined.

préjudice (FR) = harm or damage, especially in legal contexts.
prejudice (EN) = a biased opinion formed without evidence.
Latin 'praejudicium' (prior judgment) split into legal harm in French and personal bias in English.

assumer (FR) = to take responsibility for something, to own it.
assume (EN) = to suppose or take for granted.

prétendre (FR) = to claim or assert something as true.
pretend (EN) = to fake or act as if something is true when it is not.

adresser (FR) = to send or direct something to someone.
address (EN) = to deal with an issue, or to speak to a group.

délai (FR) = a deadline or time limit.
delay (EN) = a period of lateness or postponement.
One sets a boundary, the other describes slipping past it.

étiquette (FR) = a label or price tag on an object.
etiquette (EN) = rules of polite social behaviour.
At Versailles, garden signs told courtiers where to walk — the word came to mean proper conduct.

engager (FR) = to hire someone, or to commit to something.
engage (EN) = to interact with, participate in, or attract attention.

formation (FR) = training or professional development.
formation (EN) = an arrangement or structure, like a rock formation.

fournir (FR) = to supply or provide goods and services.
furnish (EN) = to equip a room with furniture.

éventuel (FR) = possible, potential — may or may not happen.
eventual (EN) = certain to happen at the end, final.

journée (FR) = the span of a full day.
journey (EN) = a trip from one place to another.
In medieval French, a 'journée' was the distance you could cover in one day — English kept only the travel part.

rester (FR) = to stay or remain in a place.
rest (EN) = to relax or stop activity.

sympathique (FR) = friendly, nice, likeable.
sympathetic (EN) = feeling compassion for someone's suffering.

sensible (FR) = sensitive, easily affected emotionally.
sensible (EN) = practical, reasonable, showing good judgment.
From Latin 'sensibilis'; French kept feeling deeply, English shifted to thinking clearly.

envie (FR) = a desire or craving for something.
envy (EN) = jealousy of what others have.
Wanting chocolate is French 'envie'; resenting someone else's chocolate is English 'envy.'

déception (FR) = disappointment, when expectations are not met.
deception (EN) = trickery, the act of lying or misleading.

décevoir (FR) = to disappoint someone.
deceive (EN) = to trick or lie to someone.

roman (FR) = a novel, a work of fiction.
roman (EN) = relating to ancient Rome or its empire.
Medieval stories written in vernacular French (not Latin) were called 'romances' — the word stuck for novels.

bras (FR) = an arm, the body part.
brass (EN) = a yellow metal alloy of copper and zinc.

limonade (FR) = a carbonated lemon-flavoured soda.
lemonade (EN) = a still drink made from fresh lemon juice, water, and sugar.
In France, ordering one gets you a fizzy soft drink, not a homemade citrus drink.

prune (FR) = a fresh plum, the fruit picked from the tree.
prune (EN) = a dried plum.
Same shift as raisin/grape: English borrowed the French word and applied it only to the dried version.

pain (FR) = bread.
pain (EN) = physical or emotional suffering.
From Latin "panis" (bread); the two words share no root despite identical spelling.

sale (FR) = dirty, filthy.
sale (EN) = a discount event, or the act of selling.
Unrelated roots: French from Frankish (dirty), English from Old Norse (selling).

table = a flat surface used for eating, working, or placing objects.
From Latin 'tabula', both languages kept the everyday furniture meaning.

orange = the citrus fruit and the colour.
The word came from Sanskrit through Arabic "naranj"; European languages dropped the initial "n" by confusing it with the article.

chocolat = the food and flavour made from cacao beans.
From Nahuatl 'xocolatl,' brought to Europe by Spanish conquistadors in the 1500s.

fruit = edible produce from a plant.
From Latin 'fructus,' both languages preserved the botanical and culinary sense.

restaurant = a place where meals are prepared and served to customers.
The word comes from French 'restaurer' (to restore) — early Parisian restaurants sold restorative broths.

zoo = a place where animals are kept for public display.
Short for 'zoological garden,' from Greek 'zoion' (animal).

appartement = a residential unit inside a building.
From Italian 'appartamento,' both languages kept the same housing meaning.

accident = an unexpected, usually harmful event.
From Latin 'accidere' (to happen), both kept the sense of chance misfortune.

information = facts or knowledge about a subject.
In French, the plural form also refers to the TV news broadcast.

communication = the exchange of information through speech, writing, or signals.
From Latin 'communicare' (to share), both preserved the same meaning.

situation = a set of circumstances or state of affairs.
From Latin 'situs' (position), both languages use it for circumstances.

solution = an answer to a problem, or a liquid mixture in chemistry.
From Latin 'solvere' (to loosen), both kept the same double meaning.

nation = a country or its people as a political community.
From Latin 'natio' (birth, people), both use it for political and cultural entities.

important = significant, having great value or influence.
From Latin 'importare' (to carry in, to matter), both languages mark what has weight.

possible = something that can happen or be done.
From Latin 'posse' (to be able), both kept the same sense.

impossible = something that cannot happen or be done.
The Latin prefix 'im-' negates 'possible' the same way in both languages.

excellent = of the highest quality or standard.
From Latin 'excellere' (to surpass), both preserved the superlative sense.

intelligent = having strong mental ability.
From Latin 'intelligere' (to understand), both describe cognitive ability.

urgent = requiring immediate attention or action.
From Latin 'urgere' (to press), both kept the sense of pressing time.

taxi = a hired car with a driver.
Short for 'taximeter' (fare meter), the word spread internationally with identical meaning.

bus = a large vehicle for carrying passengers on a fixed route.
Shortened from Latin 'omnibus' (for everyone), both languages adopted the same abbreviation.

menu = a list of available dishes in a restaurant.
From French 'menu' (detailed, small), originally meaning a detailed list of options.

animal = a living creature that is not a plant.
From Latin 'animalis' (having breath), both kept the biological category.

musique = the art of organized sound.
From Greek 'mousike' (art of the Muses), both preserved the same artistic meaning.

culture = the arts, customs, and beliefs of a society.
Also used in science for growing organisms in a controlled setting — from Latin 'cultura' (tending).

minute = a unit of time equal to 60 seconds.
From Latin 'minuta' (small part), international timekeeping aligned the unit.

seconde = a unit of time, or a position after first in a sequence.
From Latin 'secunda' (following), both kept the time and ordering meanings.

public = open to everyone, or relating to the people as a whole.
From Latin 'publicus,' both describe collective access and shared space.

total = the complete amount or sum.
From Latin 'totus' (whole), both use it for a full count.

normal = conforming to what is standard or expected.
From Latin 'norma' (carpenter's square); the measuring tool became the metaphor for what fits the rule.

national = relating to or belonging to a nation.
Built on 'nation,' the adjective stayed aligned in both languages.

international = involving two or more countries.
The prefix 'inter-' (between) worked identically in both languages.

valeur = worth in money, importance, or moral principles.
From Latin 'valere' (to be strong), both kept economic and moral meanings.

photographe = a person who takes photographs.
From Greek 'photo' (light) + 'graphe' (writing), both name the same profession.

responsable = in charge or accountable.
In French, the noun form also means manager or person in charge.

danger = a risk of harm or threat to safety.
Both languages inherited the word from Old French, keeping the sense of threat.

produit = something manufactured, grown, or created for sale.
From Latin 'producere' (to bring forth), both name what is made.

organisation = a structured group of people with a shared purpose.
From Greek 'organon' (tool, instrument), both describe coordinated bodies.

administration = the management or governance of an organization.
From Latin 'administrare' (to serve, manage), both refer to governance.

libraire (FR) = a bookseller, someone who sells books in a shop.
librarian (EN) = a person who works in a lending library, not a bookstore.

historique = relating to past events.
In French it also means "landmark" or "groundbreaking," covering both English "historical" and "historic."

économique = relating to the economy, trade, and production of wealth.
From Greek 'oikonomia' (household management), both kept the resource meaning.

magasin (FR) = a shop or store where you buy things.
magazine (EN) = a periodical publication with articles and photos.
Both come from Arabic 'makhazin' (storehouses); one stores goods, the other stores stories.

veste (FR) = a jacket, a garment with sleeves.
vest (EN) = a sleeveless undergarment or waistcoat.

large (FR) = wide or broad in size.
large (EN) = big in overall size, volume, or quantity.

formidable (FR) = wonderful, fantastic, terrific.
formidable (EN) = intimidating, inspiring fear or respect.
From Latin 'formidabilis' (fear-inspiring); French flipped it positive while English kept the original threat.

crier (FR) = to shout or yell.
cry (EN) = to weep or shed tears.

portrait = an image, painting, or description of a person.
Also used for a written character description in both languages.

garage = a building for parking or repairing vehicles.
A French word exported worldwide with identical meaning.

touriste = a person travelling for pleasure.
From French 'tour' (a journey or circuit), both describe the same traveller.

science = the systematic study of the natural world through observation and experiment.
From Latin 'scientia' (knowledge), both kept the same meaning.

architecture = the art and practice of designing buildings.
From Greek 'arkhitekton' (master builder), both preserved the construction meaning.

regarder (FR) = to watch or look at something.
regard (EN) = esteem, respect, or consideration.

caution (FR) = a security deposit or bail money.
caution (EN) = care taken to avoid danger or mistakes.
French turned it into a financial guarantee; English turned it into a warning.

réaliser (FR) = to carry out or accomplish a task or project.
realize (EN) = to become aware of something.
Modern French is absorbing the English "become aware" sense, but the core meaning remains "accomplish."

patron (FR) = the boss or employer who runs the business.
patron (EN) = a loyal customer or financial supporter.
From Latin 'patronus' (protector); French took the authority side, English took the generosity side.

pièce (FR) = a room in a home, or a coin.
piece (EN) = a part, fragment, or single item.

massage = therapeutic manipulation of muscles and soft tissue.
From Arabic 'mass' (to touch), the word entered French then spread worldwide.

surprise = an unexpected event, or the feeling of astonishment it causes.
From Old French 'surprendre' (to overtake), both kept the element of the unexpected.

concert = a live musical performance before an audience.
From Italian through French, both name the same musical event.

effort = physical or mental energy applied to a task.
From Old French 'esforcer' (to force), both kept the sense of trying hard.

virus = an infectious agent that causes disease.
Also used for harmful computer programs — Latin scientific vocabulary keeps both languages aligned.

défaut (FR) = a flaw, defect, or shortcoming.
default (EN) = the standard setting, or a failure to meet an obligation.

physicien (FR) = a physicist, a scientist who studies physics.
physician (EN) = a medical doctor.
Both trace back to Greek 'physis' (nature), but one studies nature's laws and the other heals nature's body.

propre (FR) = clean, or one's own (as in my own).
proper (EN) = correct, appropriate, or fitting.

injure (FR) = a verbal insult or offensive word.
injury (EN) = a physical wound or bodily harm.
French wounds with words; English wounds the body.

course (FR) = an errand, a race, or the act of running.
course (EN) = a series of lessons, the direction of movement, or part of a meal.

train = a railway vehicle that carries passengers or freight on tracks.
From Old French, both adopted the same transport meaning.

dialogue = a conversation between two or more people.
From Greek 'dialogos' (through-speech), both kept the conversational meaning.

costume (FR) = a men's business suit, jacket and trousers for formal wear.
costume (EN) = fancy dress, a disguise, or a stage outfit.
Boardroom in French, costume party in English.

artiste = a person who creates art — painting, music, or other creative work.
From Latin 'ars' (skill), both describe the same creative role.

avenue = a wide urban road, often lined with trees.
From French 'avenir' (to approach), both name broad city streets.

blanc (FR) = white, the colour.
blank (EN) = empty, unmarked, or without content.
Both from a Germanic root meaning bright or shining, but one became a colour and the other an absence.

joli (FR) = pretty or attractive in appearance.
jolly (EN) = cheerful, merry, full of good spirits.

retirer (FR) = to withdraw or remove — especially money from a bank.
retire (EN) = to stop working permanently at the end of a career.

prévenir (FR) = to warn or alert someone in advance.
prevent (EN) = to stop something from happening.
From Latin 'praevenire' (to come before); French warns before the event, English blocks it.

heurter (FR) = to bump into or collide with something.
hurt (EN) = to feel pain, or to cause someone pain.

silence = the complete absence of sound.
From Latin 'silentium,' both languages kept the quiet meaning.

piano = a keyboard instrument with hammers striking strings.
Short for Italian 'pianoforte' (soft-loud), naming the instrument's dynamic range.

radio = wireless audio broadcasting, or the device that receives it.
From Latin 'radius' (ray), both adopted the same shortened form.

téléphone = a device for voice communication over distance.
From Greek 'tele' (far) + 'phone' (voice), both kept the same scientific coinage.

document = an official or informative written record.
From Latin 'documentum' (lesson, proof), both kept the written record meaning.

limon (FR) = silt, fine sediment deposited by rivers.
lemon (EN) = a sour yellow citrus fruit.
Mud vs. fruit, with no shared root despite the spelling.

carte (FR) = a map, a card, or a restaurant menu.
cart (EN) = a wheeled vehicle for carrying goods.

supplier (FR) = to beg or plead desperately for mercy.
supply (EN) = to provide goods or resources.

éducation = the process of teaching, learning, and personal development.
From Latin 'educare' (to bring up, to rear), both kept the full educational meaning.

apologie (FR) = a defence or justification of something.
apology (EN) = an expression of regret for a mistake.
Defending a position in French, expressing regret in English — near opposites.

village = a small rural settlement, smaller than a town.
From Old French, both name the same type of community.

station = a stopping point for transport or services.
From Latin 'statio' (a standing place), both describe where things halt.

hôtel = a place offering paid accommodation.
In French, the word also refers to a grand private townhouse, a usage English does not share.

parc = a public green space, or an enclosed area like a theme park.
From Old French, both kept the recreational outdoor meaning.

océan = a vast body of saltwater covering much of the Earth.
Named after the Greek titan Okeanos, who personified the world-encircling river.

marcher (FR) = to walk, ordinary everyday walking.
march (EN) = to stride in a formal, military, or protest manner.

chef (FR) = a leader, boss, or head of any group or department.
chef (EN) = a professional cook, especially in a restaurant kitchen.
English borrowed the word from French but narrowed it to cooking only.

parcours (FR) = a route, path, or career journey.
parkour (EN) = an urban acrobatic sport of jumping over obstacles.
English borrowed the word specifically for the sport that emerged from French military obstacle training.

fabrique (FR) = a factory or manufacturing workshop.
fabric (EN) = textile, cloth, or woven material.
The place of making vs. what gets made.

assurance (FR) = insurance, the contract covering health, car, or home risks.
assurance (EN) = confidence, or a firm guarantee.
French also uses the word for self-confidence, but the dominant everyday meaning is the insurance policy.

ballet = a classical dance form performed to music.
Born in the Italian and French courts of the Renaissance, the word and art spread together unchanged.

continent = one of the Earth's major landmasses.
From Latin 'continere' (to hold together), both name the same geographical divisions.

résidence = a place where someone lives, especially a formal or official dwelling.
From Latin 'residere' (to sit back, settle), both kept the dwelling meaning.

profession = a skilled occupation requiring specialized training.
From Latin 'professio' (public declaration), both describe a declared career or trade.

émotion = a strong feeling such as joy, fear, or sadness.
From Latin 'emovere' (to stir up), both kept the inner experience meaning.

issue (FR) = an exit or way out, like an emergency exit.
issue (EN) = a problem, topic, or matter to discuss.
Both come from Old French "issue" meaning a way out.

achever (FR) = to finish or complete something.
achieve (EN) = to accomplish or succeed at something.
In French, the word can also mean "to finish off" (a wounded animal), but the core meaning is simply "to finish."

billet (FR) = a ticket or a banknote.
billet (EN) = temporary military lodging assigned to soldiers.

attirer (FR) = to attract or draw attention toward something.
attire (EN) = clothing or dress.

robe (FR) = a dress, an everyday garment.
robe (EN) = a bathrobe or a ceremonial gown, like a judge wears.
From Germanic 'rauba' (spoils, garments taken in war); French kept everyday dress, English kept formal or private wear.

théâtre = a venue for live performances, or the art of dramatic performance.
From Greek 'theatron' (viewing place), both kept the same dramatic meaning.

université = a higher education institution that awards degrees.
From Latin 'universitas' (community of scholars); the first ones appeared in Bologna and Paris around 1150.

page = a leaf of a book, document, or website.
From Latin 'pagina,' both kept the written surface meaning.

route = a road or path taken to reach a destination.
From Latin 'rupta via' (broken path, a road cleared through terrain), both describe a travel way.

président = the head of state, company, or organization.
From Latin 'praesidens' (sitting before, presiding), both name the person in charge.

lunettes (FR) = glasses or spectacles for seeing better.
lunatic (EN) = a crazy or insane person.
Both trace to Latin "luna" (moon): French named the round lens shape, English linked the moon to madness.

abus (FR) = misuse or excess, like drinking too much.
abuse (EN) = maltreatment or cruelty toward someone.

forfait (FR) = a flat-rate package, like a phone plan or ski pass.
forfeit (EN) = to lose something as a penalty.

rapport (FR) = a written report or official account.
rapport (EN) = a friendly, trusting relationship between people.
Paperwork in French, personal chemistry in English.

complet (FR) = full, with no room left — a full hotel or a full train.
complete (EN) = finished, whole, or entire.

guitare = a stringed instrument played by plucking or strumming.
The word travelled from Arabic 'qitara' through Spanish and French to English, unchanged in meaning.

migration = the movement of people, animals, or data from one place to another.
From Latin 'migrare' (to move), both describe the same displacement.

cinéma = a movie theatre, or the film industry as a whole.
Short for 'cinématographe,' invented by the Lumière brothers in Lyon in 1895.

catastrophe = a sudden, large-scale disaster with severe consequences.
In Greek drama, 'katastrophe' was the final turning point of a play — the meaning shifted from endings to disasters.

tunnel = an underground or underwater passage.
From Old French 'tonnelle' (barrel vault), both describe the same enclosed corridor.

brillant = extremely intelligent, or shining with bright light.
From Italian 'brillare' (to sparkle), both kept the dual meaning of shine and intellect.

esprit (FR) = mind, intellect, or wit.
spirit (EN) = soul, ghost, or an alcoholic drink.
Some overlap exists ("team spirit"), but the default meanings diverge sharply: thinking vs. soul.

gentil (FR) = kind, nice, or friendly.
gentle (EN) = soft, delicate, or mild in manner.

délivrer (FR) = to set free or liberate.
deliver (EN) = to bring a package or goods to someone.
French also uses it for issuing documents (a passport, a certificate), but the core meaning is liberation.

caractère (FR) = temperament or personality.
character (EN) = a fictional person in a story, or moral qualities.
Some overlap exists on "personality," but the default use diverges: temperament vs. fictional role.

volcan = a mountain with an opening through which lava and gases erupt.
Named after Vulcanus, the Roman god of fire and metalworking.

horizon = the line where earth and sky appear to meet.
From Greek 'horizein' (to limit), both describe the same visual boundary.

décembre = the twelfth and final month of the year.
From Latin 'decem' (ten) — it was the tenth month in the old Roman calendar before January and February were added.

guide = a person who leads others, or a book with useful information about a topic.
From Old French, both describe someone or something that shows the way.

collection = a group of gathered objects.
In fashion, it also refers to a designer's seasonal range of clothing — a usage shared by both languages.

liège (FR) = cork, the material used to seal wine bottles.
liege (EN) = a feudal lord or sovereign.

bride (FR) = a bridle, the straps used to control a horse.
bride (EN) = a woman getting married.
Reins vs. rings — no shared root despite identical spelling.

surnom (FR) = a nickname, an informal name from friends or family.
surname (EN) = a family name or last name.
Casual vs. official, despite the shared 'sur-' (over/above) prefix.

bague (FR) = a ring, jewellery worn on a finger.
bag (EN) = a container for carrying things.

dot (FR) = a dowry, money or property a bride brings to a marriage.
dot (EN) = a small round mark or point.
From Latin 'dotem' (gift); the marriage tradition shaped European alliances for centuries.

festival = a large public event centred on music, film, or culture.
From Latin 'festivalis,' both name the same joyful public gatherings.

promotion = a career advancement, or a commercial discount offer.
In French universities, the word also means a graduating class.

ambulance = an emergency vehicle that transports patients to hospital.
From French 'hôpital ambulant' (mobile hospital), the word was born on Napoleonic battlefields.

latitude = a geographical coordinate measuring distance north or south of the equator.
From Latin 'latitudo' (breadth), scientific mapping kept both languages aligned.

saxophone = a woodwind instrument with a brass body.
Named after Belgian inventor Adolphe Sax, who patented it in Paris in 1846.
